title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos Hello PRI people, Christopher here.

The other day, John mentioned Muhammad Ali’s famous line, "float like a butterfly, sting like a bee." 

It's like the PRI mantra, "Calm Up, Tense Down."

Let's practice: Let your dorsal surface float like a butterfly. Keep it soft, expansive, and light. Then, let your ventral surface sting like a bee. This is where the effort, direction, and specificity live.

When you extrapolate this to your whole body, everything changes:

* The Hand: The back of the hand and wrist stay soft; the palm-side provides the intent.
* The Head: The back of your neck is soft, the front is strong. The forehead is butterflies, the mouth is bees. Feel it. 
* The Legs: The knees float away like butterflies, while the sole-side of the foot stings down for support.

It’s a simple shift that creates a powerful balance between relaxation and precision. Next time you’re moving, try it: Float with your dorsal surface, sting with your ventral.

Here is a variation of the PRI icon to help with the exercise:

Let's practice: Let your dorsal surface float like a butterfly. Keep it soft, expansive, and light. Then, let your ventral surface sting like a bee. This is where the effort, direction, and specificity live. When you extrapolate this to your whole body, everything changes: * The Hand: The back of the hand and wrist stay soft; the palm-side provides the intent. * The Head: The back of your neck is soft, the front is strong. The forehead is butterflies, the mouth is bees. Feel it. * The Legs: The knees float away like butterflies, while the sole-side of the foot stings down for support. It’s a simple shift that creates a powerful balance between relaxation and precision. Next time you’re moving, try it: Float with your dorsal surface, sting with your ventral. title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos The Droplet becomes the Body Surfaces

John starts at the very beginning, not with anatomy, but with the simplest possible idea of a living organism: a single, soft droplet suspended in gravity. A membrane with an inside, an outside, and the constant pressures from an environment that shapes it.

Even the earliest life forms had to orient to the planet. They had to negotiate pressure from above, pressure from within, and pressure from below. That negotiation creates a surface, a kind of primordial geodesic support system, and from that comes the earliest sense of "top" and "bottom," a dorsal and ventral relationship.

PRI works from here, with the understanding that no matter how complex our bodies have become (bones, muscles, fascia, breath, behavior) we're still built on this fundamental architecture. Every body has a single surface with two different tonal qualities that manage two different forces in the same gravitational field that shaped the first organisms on Earth.

The exercises in PRI are an attempt to return to this simplicity. To understand at what a life form is, before it grows limbs or a spine or a personality, and to trace how that earliest orientation still lives in our posture today.

The Droplet becomes the Body Surfaces John starts at the very beginning, not with anatomy, but with the simplest possible idea of a living organism: a single, soft droplet suspended in gravity. A membrane with an inside, an outside, and the constant pressures from an environment that shapes it. Even the earliest life forms had to orient to the planet. They had to negotiate pressure from above, pressure from within, and pressure from below. That negotiation creates a surface, a kind of primordial geodesic support system, and from that comes the earliest sense of "top" and "bottom," a dorsal and ventral relationship. PRI works from here, with the understanding that no matter how complex our bodies have become (bones, muscles, fascia, breath, behavior) we're still built on this fundamental architecture. Every body has a single surface with two different tonal qualities that manage two different forces in the same gravitational field that shaped the first organisms on Earth. The exercises in PRI are an attempt to return to this simplicity. To understand at what a life form is, before it grows limbs or a spine or a personality, and to trace how that earliest orientation still lives in our posture today.

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos This simple image — the Archetype Tetrapod — is a visual guide to reconnecting with our ancestral support system.

This is a creature whose whole front surface rests into the ground and whose back domes gently upward into space.
And even though you’re sitting in a chair, your body is still built on that same design.

⬛ The underside (in black)
gives you weight, grounding, and support.

⬜ The upper surface (in white)
gives you openness, lift, and gentle expansion.

Those two surfaces still exist in you.
And they shape your posture more than muscular effort ever will.

🐾 Try this 1–2 minute imagery practice:

1️⃣ Imagine you’re lying like the creature in the image
(even while seated).
Picture your belly, ribs, and the front of your neck settling into the ground beneath you.

2️⃣ Let your underside feel heavy.
With each exhale, sense the whole front surface becoming warm, supported, and at ease.

3️⃣ Let your back gently rise.
Allow the upper surface to widen and lift, like the creature’s pale dome expanding upward.
No effort. Just a soft, natural spreading.

4️⃣ Hold both sensations together.
⬇️ Underside supported.
⬆️ Upper surface expanding.
Notice how your posture shifts without forcing anything.

This is one of the most direct ways to reorganize your body using imagery instead of effort.

What did you feel when you tried it?
💬 We’d love to hear what changed for you.

Allow the upper surface to widen and lift, like the creature’s pale dome expanding upward. No effort. Just a soft, natural spreading. 4️⃣ Hold both sensations together. ⬇️ Underside supported. ⬆️ Upper surface expanding. Notice how your posture shifts without forcing anything. This is one of the most direct ways to reorganize your body using imagery instead of effort. What did you feel when you tried it? 💬 We’d love to hear what changed for you.

It starts in 604 BC with Lao Tsze, who suggested that the earth is ventral and the heavens are dorsal. Fish skull development: https://www.nature.com/articles/s41598-025-91563-2 --- Maxine Sheets-Johnstone, a philosopher with a background as a teacher and professor of dance, has some articles available on the web, but the work that affects me the most is a book she has written entitled The Primacy of Movement. As a writer, she is difficult but rewarding to read. Updated May 26, 2025 7:52:32 am

Most of what's not shown in this front-view of the director is the white dorsal head and neck, and perhaps the black ventral neck between the lower jaw seams. 

Is this depiction style helpful? 
Does it do you good to imagine these changes as true for you?
Any other feedback?

I presume yes as maturity, but curious to John's experience/thoughts on this.) Seems appropriate that the dorsal expansion would allow for a "calm within the storm" in life. Our ability to withstand or weather the inevitable hurricanes of life experience and emotion. Thoughts from anybody on this?

title: Posture Release Imagery added 2 new photos.
content:
Photos Photos

It is long past time that I put something up to consider. Here, I wish to demonstrate that the relationship between the dorsal and ventral surfaces around the chest and arms gives each of the people in these photos different experiences and sends different messages. In addition, the area along the dorsal ventral seam (in this case primarily the hands) give each of the photos much of their meaning. The meaning may include caring or carefree, loving or acceptance, or what else? Subtle changes produce large effects. Try it on yourself and see how your mood changes with small physical changes. It could be that "mood" is largely "shape."

title: Legacy Exercise 41
content:
Recently, I introduced to AT Global a Posture Release Imagery principle that addresses the dorsal/ventral relationship in relation to AT’s “forward and up.” This time, and for a time or two more, I will introduce additional aspects of the relationship and more imagery to access its benefits. (The dorsal and ventral surfaces are generally poorly described in drawings by others. Having a fairly precise sense of what is dorsal and what is ventral on our bodies is important to success with PRI imagery. You will notice that more than half of the body here is shown as dorsal… and should be.)

This first illustration here shows land-bound creatures from the simplest creature that has a top and a bottom (and “prefers” upright) and has a front and a back (and predominantly moves forward). It rests ventrally on the earth. Then come the creatures “on all fours” who can support themselves further from the ground. Finally, there is us, who stand and walk while having a portion of our carriage “on edge.” (That segment of the body I call our “motor segment.” I will discuss the importance of identifying our “segments” later down the road).

I have drawn the creatures here with white dorsal surfaces and black or dark ventral surfaces. That is important in the imagery to follow. When doing the imagery exercise that follows here, endeavor to imagine/think of the white surfaces as not only white but also light and fluffy and expanding. Conversely, imagine the dark or black surfaces as also heavy, dense, and contracting.

Work to develop a good sense on your body of what is dorsal and what is from these first two illustrations. (Our entire body surface is either dorsal or ventral with a small area I call the dorsal/ventral seam). 



Next proceed to the third illustration, which is the exercise.

Proceeding from the top illustration here, spend some time imagining you have a body surface that is just the opposite of what I have been talking about. I doubt you will want to spend too long imagining this version of yourself… and I don’t blame you.

The next illustration down is like what has been recommended, and if you imagine ALL of your dorsal surface as white, fluffy, light, and expanding while imaging ALL of your ventral surface as dark, dense, and contracting, you will be much happier and sense a much better Use.
 

The last two illustrations are intensifiers of what you just imagined and can provide greater insights into a habit structure you may carry about too often. Notice in the next illustration your ventral surface has full contact and support from the earth, like the earliest of organisms on land enjoyed. In fact, since the ventral surface is imagined contracting, it can be thought of as not only resting on the earth but also embracing it. Remember, there is nothing to do here except imagine.

The final illustration suggests that your ventral surface has become the earth and your dorsal surface has expanded to fill everywhere above. Don’t expect success yet with this last version of this exercise at this point. But maybe you will surprise me.

title: Posture Release Imagery added 4 new photos.
content:
Photos Photos Photos Photos

Maybe some of you will find these earliest drawings of mine interesting and funny. I never was an artist and these drawings show that I hadn't figured out much yet either. But I was beginning to. I think they were drawn close to 25 years ago for the purpose of adding a little interest (evolution and postural types) to lecture/demonstrations of the Alexander Technique. I have others packed away somewhere that I will some day put up... and they are equally funny or funnier.

title: Posture Release Imagery added 2 new photos.
content:
Photos Photos

Followers should recall images like these... showing a lion and a human each enjoying the benefits of total ventral support. I have drawing several versions of a ventral support "chair" but never got around to building one. I wish that I had... since I strained my shoulder rather badly recently and discovered as part of the treatment and cure for the injury that supporting my ventral surface of my whole body (as well as that of my shoulder) as much as easily possible is a definite good. Sleeping at night in the traditional manner for the last few nights has not been easy. I experience more pain at night than during the day. So I built a makeshift ventral support chair by taking a traditional straight-backed chair and sitting backwards on the outer edge with my legs curled around the back of it... and various pillows and two rolled up sleeping bags to support my stomach, chest, arms, chin, and backs of legs. It is very comfortable when I get it adjusted and have fallen asleep  pain-free or at least eliminated the pain if not fallen asleep. Proof to me that we are designed not to experience any dorsal tension except when we desire to... and that a bed does not always afford us that experience. Sort of a long-winded description/discussion... but it is because I am very happy with its effect.

title: Posture Release Imagery updated their status.
content: It may be the same at any age, but when one gets older (I can attest), it is the presence of both peace and liveliness in the limbs that is most desired. The core feels at home but It is in movement... and in new challenging movement.... that the limbs and edges of the body expose their habit and brittleness. Perhaps it is a careless assertion to say that I have mastered most all of my gross habitual behaviors, but I am currently finding more value in teasing out the rigidity and flaccidity that lives along my dorsal-ventral seam. Soon, I hope, I will complete a new Note on type-specific and other imaginary manipulation of the seam. Stay tuned.

title: Posture Release Imagery added 2 new photos.
content:
Photos Photos

Here is a chart showing how to experience the four dorsal-ventral types by simply (well, it is a bit of work) imagining the different body portions are either dorsally lengthening and ventrally widening or the opposite. Imagining the two side expanding in different directions strongly enhances shape change without effort. This gives you a chance (if YOU give IT a chance) to experience the four postural/personality types (without gender differentiation)... perhaps more easily than with some of the other type-specific imagery. For some more discussion on this subject, you can look at my current posting of this illustration and the discussion that goes with it here: https://www.facebook.com/groups/AlexanderTechniqueGlobal The second illustration is a simple way of experiencing the value of thing about widening as well as lengthening (when you want to touch your toes or do a back bend).

title: Posture Release Imagery updated their status.
content: Jimmy League O'theirown, https://www.facebook.com/jim.dugan.167 , has posted this question that I am putting up front here since I think it is a great one and others could easily add to it. "How would you teach posture release imagery to young children? I'm wondering how you would explain concepts like the dorsal and ventral surfaces, or the idea of tensed and relaxed muscles. Would you change the images to be more palatable to youngsters?" Jim, I've ruminated about your question here too long. It has some rather easy answers, though incomplete, to it. Let my comments here be like the beginning of brain-storming for me, you, and all others interested. No final pronouncements coming from me. Ok, not sure what age group you exactly have in mind but my first thought is that PRI's imagery (and through the back door, its concepts) might best involve plastering as large as possible versions of all kinds of its drawings on the walls (which would be better than a screen... more "real" that way). Maybe they could get names, like PRIMO, the heroic man/woman, PRIMO 2, for a heroic dog or other tetrapod, and PRIMO 3, for a loveable and healthy alligator sort. Then the archetypes of the types could be named as well... Lacy/Larry LaidBack, Jumpy Jim/Jenny, and so forth. I could go on but no. I think the drawings or new drawings could explain the dorsal-ventral thing a lot more easily than you might think. Kids aren't looking for scientific evidence, I don't think. One could explain that the two surfaces on our heroes fell like a trampoline on the ventral side and like a billowing, fluffy, light cover on the dorsal side. Playing roles or having kids pretend to be ("inside," not necessarily act out at first) the various possibilities. Which do they like the most. What do they not like. (That introduces the fact that they are affected... changed by the imagery... without having to get too heady about it. Play with changing the nature/appearance of the d/v seam and other stuff. I'll stop here and hope more ideas are added. Thanks, Jim.

title: Posture Release Imagery updated their status.
content: Below is a private conversation I've been having with Danny Lie that he agreed could go onto the page. I communicate with followers every so often and I always think the conversation may help others working to be understand PRI and have success with its imagery. So Danny agrees that would be a good thing. This starts following introductory comments we shared and continues here. It may continue in the comment section and you others can enter as well. Danny wrote: … Your posture release imagery has helped me an awful lot to be more conscious about my posture and the way I move. Especially during running, focusing on the dorsal/ventral seam and separate director/motor/rudder segments has been tremendously helpful to relax my shoulders and move more fluently. It is very kind of you to freely share this valuable information. Thanks again! Danny Lie John A.: Thanks for your comments and info about how PRI helps you...  (and a bit more). See you around, John John August Appleton P.S. Have you played successfully with any of the type-specific imagery? Just wondering if you have. Danny: In my mind 1B is the preferred type but without conscious thought I usually revert to the less desirable 2A. I haven't given the other types much thought yet, but I can clearly see all these types in the people around me. I think 1B is very common among young children, but rare in adults John A.: My favorite is 1B and my "less desirable" is 2A, as well. The other types contain good and useful differences but some undesirable qualities for me. I can see how you might consider 1B more of a universal ideal than just your opposite. But I must say that people who are stuck in the 1B form might think just the opposite... that 2A is ideal. We frequently love our opposites (and that is what makes the world go around). People's types are not always that easy to see others or sense in ourselves. But I will admit that adults collapse in ways that children do not so often... and they may do so by collapsing into our dominant style even if they are actually 1Bs. The whole type thing is complicated by so many other influences but I am still personally certain that it exists. Have you ever tried imagining that the right side of your body is a 1A type and the left side is a 2B ? Use any of the type-specific imagery to do this with. It can be very good for our habitual types, 2A, since people have some opposite tendencies on their left from their right sides. They are not perfectly equal in how they work at all. This is enough. Thanks for asking. Best wishes. Danny: I really like to focus on your images and ideas during skating, walking and running. And your right: it is very complicated. More than enough stuff to ponder for a lifetime! I particularly like the image of a leaping creature in relaxed preparation for a soft landing (1B). However, I think focusing on the landing as an event hinders fluid motion. Instead I try to imagine a fluid endless motion without separate time-segments between landing, support, push-off and glide. Upon ground contact I like to image the moving road simply picks up my feet and takes them backward. That is why I struggle a bit with the qualities direct and hard for type 1B. I would like to be indirect and soft right before I touch the ground. My mind is ahead while my peripheral thought and vision is on the ground below. During walking and running there is a distinct opposite posture and movement pattern between the left and right side which resonates nicely with your ideas of opposite qualities. I also began looking for the link between martial arts postures and movement and your imagery, but I've only just begun practicing tai chi and kung fu. Have you ever considered the significance of spinal rotation? I think fluid motion is not possible without the spine rotating around its axis. Enough ideas! Danny: I have been thinking more about your waves and they might be more complicated. Your arms and legs are cross linked (left arm to right leg and vice versa) and during gait, the dorsal surface between the trailing shoulder and hip contracts, while the surface between the moving forward arm/shoulder and hip/leg expands, creating a saddle figure. The same happens on your ventral surface in opposite fashion and this is accompanied by a rotation of the spine in the middle of your torso at the middle point of the oscillating 'saddle'. John (new as of this posting): Many subjects and interesting comments and questions you bring up. First, I want to put PRI in perspective. Though it helps considerably with movement, it is not generally what is called motor imagery. It is more related to creating non-habitual and archetypal form or structures that movement takes place within. So, in other words, you are definitely free if it is helpful to create some movement in the imagined types to help you along. They are not solid forms. They are made of rubbery material (haha) that have inclinations to return to a particular type's archetypal form. If you "struggle a bit with the qualities of direct and hard"for type 1B" [part of the qualities that make up what Danny thinks is his opposite type] then forget them for the moment. Actually, since we have opposite type influences that exist habitually on each of our sides, your left side (if 1B is your "opposite") is a 2B, which is experienced with a sense of softness and indirectness. Could that be where you most dislike the "hard" and "direct" qualities introduced? As for your last paragraph about rotation of the spine and oscillating spine, first, yes, spiraling of our form around itself (even without reference to a spine) certainly happens in natural movement. I experience it. I don't think that it particularly needs to be encouraged nor do I think that the imagery discourages that movement, especially when experimenting with the specific opposite types having some influence on opposite sides. I do think that diagonal contracting of the dorsal surface in walking or running should not be strongly felt. There may be some "oscillating 'saddle' " effect as you suggest but that too much of that sense should not be there, if at all. Maybe diagonal lengthening on the ventral side and diagonal widening on the dorsal side is what is called for. All difficult stuff to talk about... but worth it. Cheers.

title: Posture Release Imagery updated their status.
content: To followers and visitors, I have decided to more consciously avoid placing new thoughts or imagery on this Facebook page. I don’t receive enough feedback for it to continue to be enjoyable or rewarding to do so. Any of you who is sufficiently interested in commenting or asking any questions can contact me privately (see About / Contact Info on this page). I happily give time to personal emails. I am still… and will always be… personally engrossed in what Posture Release Imagery does for me and can do for others. I am continuing to work on the book, tentatively titled, “Calm Up and Tensed Down… and other tension releasing & posture forming imagery”. The book is about three-quarters complete but I'm uncertain how it will be published and made available. I suspect that the only way that PRI has a chance of enduring is if some very interested individuals, using the book, become very familiar with the principles and successful with its range of exercises. So, this is where I am going to be putting my hopes and efforts. Sincerely, John

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos Some of you may have seen this photo before. The squirrel is being exuberant, spiritual, or desirous... or maybe getting ready to catch a friend in need. But despite what reason, he/she is doing it in a healthy way. Many of us, if we were to do the same thing with such gusto, would wrench our back in some manner. What is the squirrel doing right that many humans don't?

A click on the link below will get you to my suggested explanation. It can also be used as an imagery exercise for when you want to let it loose like this (which I hope you do... because life is too amazing not to have this reaction on some occasions).

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos This chart and the links below (image exercises I wrote for my website a couple or more years ago) are put here because they make it rather easy to begin to experience the four types kinesthetically, structurally, and hopefully even emotionally or psychologically. If you stay with the imagery long enough (which involves only imagining your director and rudder segments in these funny waving states) you can experience a whole body of change. Just LIGHTLY shake out your body occasionally as you imagine the various new features and you will experience a world of change, good change. Only one of the type's will be unrewarding (your own).

Here is the related imagery:

http://posturereleaseimagery.org/seemingly-silly-seriously-significant/ 

http://posturereleaseimagery.org/seemingly-silly-part-2/ 

http://posturereleaseimagery.org/seemingly-silly-part-3/

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos Folks,
Here is an old PRI image exercise that may be somewhere online, but I don't remember where. It is useful and educational for those seeking to bend over in a healthy way to pick something up, for instance. (Actually "fold" is a better, less habit-ridden term than "bend over").

People familiar with the Alexander Technique frequently call it 'going into "monkey".' (By practicing it a few times you will discover why.)

Follow the directions here and imagine the bizarre stages. It causes you to avoid collapsing tendencies and keeps that which is facing up... light. And that is the basic PRI dorsal-ventral principle in action.

This is another image that suggests the opposite of what you think you are doing, like PRI's "imagine you are standing when sitting and imagine you are sitting when standing" image.

Over time you will continually improve at the "monkey," noticing especially where you are dorsally holding or tensing... and being able to go deeper into the fold... towards the floor.

Don't forget the last part... returning to standing position. That's fun.

Folks, Here is an old PRI image exercise that may be somewhere online, but I don't remember where. It is useful and educational for those seeking to bend over in a healthy way to pick something up, for instance. (Actually "fold" is a better, less habit-ridden term than "bend over"). People familiar with the Alexander Technique frequently call it 'going into "monkey".' (By practicing it a few times you will discover why.) Follow the directions here and imagine the bizarre stages. It causes you to avoid collapsing tendencies and keeps that which is facing up... light. And that is the basic PRI dorsal-ventral principle in action. This is another image that suggests the opposite of what you think you are doing, like PRI's "imagine you are standing when sitting and imagine you are sitting when standing" image. Over time you will continually improve at the "monkey," noticing especially where you are dorsally holding or tensing... and being able to go deeper into the fold... towards the floor. Don't forget the last part... returning to standing position. That's fun.

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos All of the following is conjecture about tendencies concerning the body and us. It is NOT absolute... just thoughts interesting to me... and maybe to you:

Calm and ease are primarily a gift to the dorsal surface.
 
Thought has its primary connection with locations along the dorsal-ventral seam... we see or hear what someone is say (including ourselves) and we get "in touch" with others and ideas as well. It is where we feel most alive in normal daily activity. 

Pleasure is a gift received and embraced on the ventral side. 

Things go wrong in different ways. For example when we do not release habitual tension on the dorsal surface, which makes us unhappy, we may 1. strain our mind all of the way out to the source of our senses (along the d-v seam) to do something about it or 2. misuse or overuse our pleasure zones on the ventral surface to take us away from our misuse... our pain and distresses. 

Does this engage anyone?

Does this engage anyone?

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos Here is a chart that I don't think I've revealed yet... at least this completely. It is an "advanced" level chart, for the experienced, but maybe it will inspire others to learn more PRI basics. If you can imagine all of the elements indicated here (in a fairly short time period before previous elements imagined have faded away) you will experience some significant "ground breaking" changes, especially if imagining your opposite type.

The white areas are dorsally full and fluffy areas. The vertically represented "bear rug" characters represent the dorsal view (on the left) and the ventral view with black areas - seen THRU the dorsal side, which is made invisible. And the bottom vertical "bear rug" versions represent the added and opposing influences that each type has on each side. 

One thing that is strongly suggested here is that flowing movement does not require imagining flowing lines within the body such as seen in the curvy creatures shown near the top of the chart. Imagining "excessive" freedom and, in other locations, close contact between adjacent body segments... brings about graceful movement that doesn't sacrifice lightness, which is maintained by the structure inherent in the segments. (I hope some of you get what I mean here).

Questions?

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos This is for advanced followers. 

In response to a discussion on this earlier post, https://www.facebook.com/posturereleaseimagery/posts/980915478605411 , I decided to create this complex chart. It takes studying. Perhaps nothing is too obvious in it.

The chart shows caricatures of the four type made eight... by showing the distinctions of the male and female versions. The creatures with the dorsal-ventral wave are the most recognizable, I am sure... but they are drawn in two, somewhat different versions, to indicate imagining the various ones with greater emphasis on the ventral surface or the dorsal surface. The other creatures are from the peristaltic wave types, in this case with an additional entirely horizontal version illustrated... in order to make them easy to compare with the dorsal-ventral wave types.

What should be understood is that tonal patterns (that are experienced through imagery) can and do exist in the presence of other patterns. Each of our specific type's dorsal-ventral wave patterns exist within us along with our type's peristaltic wave type. And some predictable dorsal-ventral wave differences on each of us also are present. ALSO, there are numerous other influences on us that make their home in our flesh. It might seem, therefore, that we are hopelessly complex when seeking to untangle some of our habits. I would say, yes, we are complex... but not hopelessly so.

See what you can make of thinking and imagining various qualities in the same type, one right after another. Things to note: all of the creatures are facing to the right. The horizontal peristaltic type are place above or below the male or female version of the dorsal-ventral wave types that they match. What looks like heads separated from bodies in the peristaltic types are to imagined as the whole director segment... meaning that the neck and half of the shoulder and arms are to be included in the particular tonal shape indicated.

What looks like heads separated from bodies in the peristaltic types are to imagined as the whole director segment... meaning that the neck and half of the shoulder and arms are to be included in the particular tonal shape indicated.

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos Any of you followers who at least partially understand and have experienced the waves (and types) within us will understand that it is logical that the mouth of this flattened milk jug (which was NOT also flattened by my feet) would consistently show a flattened quality... in an OPPOSITE direction.

I have flattened (and recycled) dozens or probably hundreds of these plastic jugs and they always produce this picture. Every force or intention you apply on your body "seeks" to influence adjoining body segments in opposite ways. Its built into us.

title: Posture Release Imagery added a new photo.
content:
Photos The dogma of Posture Release Imagery is in its principles.  Without knowing those principles and understanding their meaning, the imagery here is fairly dead-end. It will work but will not substantially help you over the years. It will become less sharp and distorted.

Of course, those principles should be tested, not only personally but also in a broader scientific manner. Time will tell if that happens. I pray that that happens, because PRI, in my estimation, is considerably more than just imagery that works well for some and not for others. It is the beginnings of a “science of sensation”… and sensation’s direct effects on structure and movement. PRI is also among the sciences of origin. It postulates the origin of sensation in the earliest of organisms that gathered data on shape or form as the means to our own improved structures and movement. 

So, if Posture Release Imagery interests you, I suggest you explore it deeply, not just for immediate cessation of discomforts or a gratification of some sort. A greater understanding of your postural and movement habits as well as your own personality and that of others awaits you. Finally a deepening of spiritual or religious understanding is also possible.

So, I recommend that followers, new or old, either dig deeper into PRI or give it up entirely. Skimming off of the top of things is a modern disease that at least the internet fosters. I know myself of this tendency. 

Feel free to write with comments or questions. I may not offer too much more on this Facebook page, but I will always respond to questions and substantial comments.

John
